title: SEGMENT 13 — Sustainable manufacturing: replacing petrochemicals with engineered microbes

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Sustainable manufacturing: replacing petrochemicals with engineered microbes
Step back and notice the scale of the chemical industry.

The petrochemical industry produces, every year, hundreds of millions of tons of bulk chemicals — ethylene, propylene, butadiene, benzene, acrylic acid, ethylene glycol, the building blocks of nearly every plastic, fiber, and synthetic material in modern life.
The feedstock is petroleum or natural gas.
The processes are energy-intensive.
The carbon ends up, eventually, in landfills or in the atmosphere as CO2.
The whole pipeline is a major source of greenhouse gas emissions and a primary reason chemistry is sometimes called "the dirty cousin" of the energy industry.
Metabolic engineering offers an alternative, in principle.

Engineered microbes, fed on sugar from agricultural waste or — eventually — on CO2 directly, can produce many of the same molecules, or close substitutes for them, at room temperature in water-based fermenters.
The challenge is economics.
Microbial production has to compete with extremely cheap, extremely optimized petrochemical processes, and for most commodity chemicals it cannot.

The successful examples — and there are real ones — tend to be molecules where either microbial production has a unique chemistry advantage, or where there is a regulatory or consumer push toward bio-based alternatives that justifies the price premium.
Let me give you a few of the success stories.
Lactic acid.
Lactic acid is one of the great commercial success stories of microbial production.
It is made by lactic acid bacteria — Lactobacillus, Lactococcus — and the engineering involves selecting and optimizing strains for high yield and high product tolerance.
The product market is enormous and growing, because lactic acid is the monomer for polylactic acid, or PLA, a biodegradable plastic used in packaging, fibers, and increasingly in medical implants.
Companies like NatureWorks — a joint venture between Cargill and others — produce hundreds of thousands of tons of PLA per year from corn-derived sugars via lactic acid fermentation.
This is one of the largest microbial production processes in the world, and it competes effectively with petrochemical plastics on price for certain market segments.
1,4-butanediol is a classic case of using metabolic engineering to access a molecule that does not occur in nature.
Butanediol is a precursor to spandex, certain plastics, and many specialty chemicals.

Genomatica, a San Diego-based company, engineered E. coli to produce butanediol from sugar by stitching together a non-natural pathway — a sequence of enzymes that, in this combination, does not exist in any organism.

They identified candidate enzymes through pathway prediction software and biochemical screening, assembled the pathway in E. coli, optimized it through many rounds of strain engineering, and licensed the technology to BASF and Novamont for commercial production.
Bio-based butanediol is now a real commercial product, and several other Genomatica molecules — butadiene, caprolactam — are in various stages of commercialization.
Succinic acid is a four-carbon dicarboxylic acid used as a feedstock for many polymers, plasticizers, and specialty chemicals.

Several companies — Reverdia, Succinity, BioAmber — have built engineered microbial processes for succinate at industrial scale, in some cases using yeast (which tolerates the low pH that comes with high carboxylic acid concentrations better than E. coli does).
The economics have been mixed; some of these companies have struggled financially, but the technology works.
Isobutanol and other higher alcohols are being pursued as next-generation biofuels and as chemical intermediates.

Gevo and several others have engineered E. coli and yeast to produce isobutanol via the keto acid pathway — a clever bit of metabolic engineering that diverts the cell's amino acid biosynthesis machinery to produce alcohol instead of amino acids.
Yields and economics remain challenging, but the engineering itself has been demonstrated to work.

Adipic acid, the precursor to nylon, has been a holy grail for sustainable chemistry because the conventional petrochemical process emits enormous quantities of nitrous oxide, a potent greenhouse gas.
Several engineering efforts — most notably Verdezyne, before it shut down — pursued microbial adipate.
The economics did not, in that case, quite work out, but the engineering progress was significant and the goal remains active.
The analogy I want you to carry: sustainable manufacturing via metabolic engineering is the project of moving the chemical industry off petroleum and onto fermentable feedstocks.
Lactic acid for biodegradable plastics.
1,4-butanediol for fibers.
Succinate for polymers.
Isobutanol for fuel and chemicals.

Each one is a fight against the entrenched petrochemical cost structure, and the wins, when they come, depend on a combination of unique chemistry, environmental policy pressure, and consumer demand for bio-based products.
The wins are real but partial.
The dream of a fully bio-based chemical industry is, as of the mid-2020s, still a dream, but the foundations under it are getting steadily stronger.
There is a particular sub-thread worth noting: the carbon source itself is becoming a frontier.
Most of the engineered processes today use sugar — corn-derived glucose in the US, sugarcane-derived sucrose in Brazil, sometimes glycerol from biodiesel byproducts.
These sugars compete with food production for arable land, which raises real concerns about land use and food security if microbial chemistry scales to bulk-chemical volumes.

The next-generation feedstocks the field is targeting include lignocellulosic sugars from agricultural waste (which requires solving the difficult enzymatic breakdown of plant cell walls), methanol (which can be produced from natural gas or biomass and supports engineered methylotrophs like Pichia or Methylobacterium), and ultimately CO2 itself, captured directly from the atmosphere or from industrial flue gas.
We will close the lecture with the recent CO2-fixing E. coli work, which is the most striking demonstration of what carbon-neutral biomanufacturing might one day look like.
Now let us turn to a wave that has been even more visible to consumers in the last few years — alt-protein and the engineered production of food ingredients that used to come from animals.
